chapters.title: Riding the rails of change
chapters.text:
Imagine sitting in the second car of a giant roller coaster. It's too late to turn back now, right? The beginning of a significant turning point isn't the time to take risks — it's the time to focus on what’s essential. The same goes for business. Navigating the business world is like riding that coaster’s breathtaking twists and turns, mind-blowing acceleration, terrifying drops, and hair-raising loops. Each of these adrenaline-evoking events, or inflection points, develops through four stages:

• Hype: This is when the guy in front puts his hands up to let people know something big is coming. He knows what he’s doing, so you invest (or let go of the lap bar).
• Dismissive: You’ve survived the shakeout (ride), so you begin to set a foundation for growth by looking for other coasters — finding new ways to meet customer needs, building business models, and exploring investment opportunities.
• Emergent: This stage is relatively calm, like the end of the ride. It's when you realize how inflection points work and start creating options to prepare for the emergence of a new model.
• Maturity: Once you understand that a certain inflection point will change your world, you will be well-positioned to take advantage of its growth opportunities (or search for more ways to get your adrenaline pumping).

Inflection points have the power to change the very assumptions on which organizations were founded. ~ Rita Gunther McGrath

Are you ready to conquer the business world coaster like a champ? Stamp your ticket and buckle up!

chapters.title: Expand your peripheral vision
chapters.text:
The changes that will fundamentally influence the future of any organization brew at the edges — the frontiers beyond which something changes. Daily demands put pressure on many business executives, causing them to prioritize reaching the periphery as one of the last things on their agenda. However, knowing what is happening is essential to avoid being surprised by an inflection point.

To stay updated as a business leader, build an effective feedback system.

Major strategic inflection points often take a while to develop and fully reveal their effects. Although they may not be apparent at first, it's possible to discern their future implications with careful observation while you can still make an impact. Eight practices can assist in identifying developments at the fringes:

• Ensure there is a clear link between the leaders and the employees at the borders of your organization. Have people who can tell you what is really going on.
• Consider a wide range of viewpoints when contemplating the future impact of your business.
• Use deliberate decision-making processes for high-risk and irreversible decisions that have enormous implications for your business. Create small, empowered teams to make reversible decisions that hold great learning potential.
• Create little educational bets that everyone in the organization can participate in. This encourages people to be more creative and express their ideas even if they are not part of management.
• Have direct exposure to the environment by getting to know how your customers feel about your business or products. You can do this by situating leaders where they can easily run into clients.
• Create incentives that reveal accurate information about your business.
• Keep a keen eye out for any negative impact your organization may have on the public.
• Make sure to position yourself and your organization where the future is taking shape.

You don't have to hold a managerial role in your company to recognize how a turning point is likely to happen. You will notice it if you remain closely connected to the external trends that enable your business.

chapters.title: The key to business survival
chapters.text:
They say change is the only constant in life. As time passes, things change; valuable things become obsolete, and things that were once unimportant begin to shine through. One of the most significant effects of this change phenomenon is on businesses and market structure in general.

Businesses are built and survive on predominant realities, and any change that affects these realities will inevitably lead to an inflection in said business. For instance, a few years ago, TV stations and newspaper publishers had the largest audiences, so the publicity and information circulation business built up around them (Hype Inflection Point).

Today, that narration has changed with the emergence of social media platforms such as Facebook, YouTube, and Instagram. The inflection arose when social media subtly and gradually altered the assumption upon which TV stations and newspaper publishers built their businesses. (Dismissive Inflection Point) Consequently, this assumption changed when media houses discovered they could reach a more significant and diverse audience through social media. (Emergent Inflection Point) Companies that took advantage of that inflection point had a competitive advantage. (Maturity Inflection Point)

It is easy to take inflection points for granted, often because of a blurred business vision and inadequate forecasting. Remember that roller coaster? It really didn’t look that daunting from afar, did it? However, the consequences of an underestimated minor change often result in the inflection adversely affecting the business.

Your business’ success hinges on how you adapt to your customer needs.

While trying to establish the inescapable and constant nature of change, we must not fail to add that some things never change in business. For example, the needs and preferences of consumers are always constant. The day consumers desire higher prices for your goods or wish you were less effective in rendering your services will never come. So, what changes in business are the means and approaches to meeting consumers’ needs rather than the needs themselves. And preparation is vital to maximize the opportunities and changes that are right around the bend. In other words, the coaster really doesn’t change. But how you approach it and where you sit alters your whole experience.

chapters.title: Rethink to thrive
chapters.text:
Businesses generally thrive on certain beliefs, which is the logic behind their prosperity. For instance, restaurants run on the assumption that people are always hungry, and as long as this remains true, they'll always be in business. However, it is crucial always to review the assumptions that serve as the back-end of your business to see if they need a little adjusting, tweaking, or a total overhaul.

What kills many businesses is they don't recognize the warning signs of a diminishing advantage. As a result, they seldom notice that the assumptions upon which their business stands are gradually becoming obsolete. If it is true that people are always hungry, is it also true that they'd be willing to eat just anything at any time? These and many more assumptions may have been valid several years ago but may not be applicable in the current setting.

Knowing your customers will always keep you ahead of the game.

Staying current in the business sphere requires that you reassess whether your assumptions about your company need a new perspective. Often, consumers go through a lot of stress sourcing all the materials they need to get a particular job done. A simple decision to make available for sale all or most of these materials may be the initiation of a significant inflection that will work in your favor. For instance, as a restaurant owner, you can decide to start selling soda or dessert if you discover that your customers always go out to look for these after eating at your place.

Next, review your business advantages to check if they are fading away. You can do this by defining the revenues your business relies on, identifying the needs your customers are willing to spend money on, and how your business might change given shifts in the environment and new possibilities.

A powerful source of inspiration with respect to the next inflection point is to consider what gets in the way of customers achieving their goals. ~ Rita McGrath

chapters.title: All hands on deck!
chapters.text:
Have you ever noticed that, as consumers, we frequently place the entire responsibility for a business's success or failure on the owners and top leaders? We blame them when the company fails and praise them when it succeeds. Meanwhile, in the real sense, the organization's heroes are those who face the heat of the job, interact directly with the public, and know exactly what is going on at all times.

You will promote a growth mindset when you give people the chance to spot change and act on it.

We cannot say that a business succeeds or fails because of any particular hierarchy of individuals. It is due to the combination and predictions of the various categories of personnel running the business. While the staff members who interact with the public bring insights based on their observations, the leader's role is to create a means of receiving and scrutinizing this feedback to determine what is important. Afterward, they assign the responsibility of handling these issues to those most qualified.

Your business is most galvanized to survive inflection points when all your workers have a shared point of view and you've taken steps to eliminate every form of internal friction and competition. So, it is vital to mobilize actions to take advantage of this opportunity by empowering everyone in your company to spot inflection points and do something about them. So, it goes without saying that if your business were to neglect the feedback gathered by your frontline workers, the efficiency of your business would decline, and your organization would no longer be able to make sound and timely decisions.

Did you know? According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 20% of small businesses in the United States fail within the first year. After their fifth year, nearly 50% have faltered. Ten years later, only about a third of businesses have survived.

chapters.title: Leading through inflections
chapters.text:
Recognizing and responding to turning points cannot be accomplished by the individual in the executive suite; everyone must be involved. Leaders who facilitate relationships and coordinate talents while providing clear direction can successfully guide their businesses through inflection points.

Teamwork, inclusivity, mutual respect, and collaboration comprise the bedrock of a positive workplace.

According to Executive Chairman of Amazon, Jeff Bezos, recognizing that an unavoidable change is coming is easy. (We must get on the coaster because we already bought tickets, and we are next in line.) Anticipating that change’s impact and deciding which path to take is the hard part. (We know the last seat gives the wildest ride, the first is the scariest, and the middle is the calmest. So, which section should we choose?) It is also about carrying everyone through an inflection point and coming out stronger on the other side. (Our group will sit together, so can everyone handle the back of the coaster? Or should I choose more conservatively? We are all a part of this adventure, so I must make it fun for all.) Moreover, considering the future advantages of your decisions, despite the potential short-term challenges, is essential. Recognizing a turning point is the initial stage in effectively managing it. Subsequently, determining the course of action and rallying the company is necessary. However, ensure key leaders share the same perspective and promote collaboration; a lack of agreement will dilute your endeavors. 

Gail Goodman's leadership in the email marketing company Constant Contact led to incremental progress. Here are a few takeaways from Goodman's leadership style:

• Act as a cohesive force that binds varied employees to strive towards a clearly defined goal consistently.
• Place importance on candid feedback to remain effective.
• Communicate your company's mission, vision, values, priorities, and critical themes constantly. This communication shouldn't be done only at the top; involve everyone in your company.

Your primary duty is to ensure that your team stays united. If your team is not working well together, it's your responsibility as a leader to unite them. Create an environment where the team can resolve their conflicts peacefully. Designate times when you can give them your undivided attention and truly listen to them. Ensure your team members are actively listening, and immediately address the issue if they are not.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Taking advantage of inflection points to become better and more efficient is not just a concept for organizations and businesses to embrace. You must prepare yourself on a personal level for experiences and changes that would affect the course of your life. These experiences often come from the most unexpected places, hence the need to adjust your perception of them beforehand. Also, plan on navigating through them such that you end up in the direction you want to be heading. Remember, inflection points will alter your business and personal lives according to how you prepare and approach them.

Try this

• Reflect on the last time a major change happened in your life. How could you have prepared and handled it differently?
• Attend a conference or seminar unrelated to your business. Note how others set their businesses up to navigate change.
• Watch a TED talk on preparing for change.
